text: Hello, I'm sharing an interesting case of a patient with long standing history of abdominal pain with obstipation followed by explosive bowel movements and resolution of discomfort. This would be associated with massive abdominal distension.
and had had scopes of which the most recent one couldn't get through and then was sent for this outpatient CAT scan at a time that he felt absolutely fine.
The surgeon visually identifies a volvulus located at the splenic flexure of the descending colon, highlighting the twisted segment of the bowel.
And so I took him to the operating room. This is actually an elective procedure. The patient was absolutely fine, could not tolerate the bowel prep, did not go through.

I was concerned about the amount of distension, but it was only in the upper abdomen. So with a kind of a lower doc, I decided to go ahead and, Just get in there and then decompress the bowel as I often will do in cases of toxic distension of the colon or large bowel obstruction.

The surgeon uses a Tuo silk suture to create a purse string around the dilated colon, causing it to deflate immediately as air escapes, revealing no stool present. The suture is then tied to secure the decompression.

The surgeon traces the bowel to locate the twist, identifying the transverse colon extending towards the splenic flexure. Visible scarring in the mesentery indicates chronic twisting. As the surgeon pulls on the bowel, it detours, highlighting the affected area.

The surgeon brings the small bowel over the ligament of Treitz to view the mesentery unobstructed. The descending colon is traced down to the sigmoid colon, revealing a very attenuated white line with no retroperitoneal attachments. The bowel is completely detorsed, and the surgeon prepares for a generous resection of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon continues to decompress the bowel, reducing its size to facilitate easier manipulation during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the camera in the midline, observing the unsplayed mesentery without any twisting. The camera setup is adjusted from two lefts to two rights for better visualization.

The surgeon examines the mesentery, noting areas of twisting and scarring. They trace the bowel to ensure accuracy and mark the planned distal margin on the distal descending colon, preparing for potential further resection if necessary.

The surgeon dissects the bowel wall from a medial position, carefully following underneath it due to the absence of lateral attachments. The mesentery and the white line are taken together during this process. The camera is positioned in the patient's right lower quadrant, providing a clear view of the mesentery.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection of the bowel, holding it up with arms one and four while using a vessel sealer in arm three. The dissection follows closely along the bowel wall without requiring high ligations, avoiding proximity to the spleen and major vessels. The surgeon encounters a colotomy during the procedure.

The surgeon performs several firings of the vessel sealer through the thick mesentery and parietal peritoneum tissue near the descending colon. The spleen is visible in the distance, and the splenic flexure is seen curving below its usual position, facilitating an easy takedown of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon continues to maneuver around the corner, visually inspecting the attachment of the omentum to the transverse colon. The surgeon aims to enter the lesser sac and identify the margin on the transverse colon, although this step has not yet been reached.

The surgeon approaches the gastrocolic omentum anteriorly, focusing on the distal transverse colon. The omentum is visible as the surgeon attempts to identify the lesser sac, taking care to separate the omentum from the mesentery while navigating around the corner of the transverse colon.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the transverse colon, progressing towards the falciform ligament. The omentum is unfolded and positioned to ensure it lays without tension, confirming the inclusion of two colotomies in the dissection.
The surgeon marks the orientation on the mesentery and proceeds to dissect through it at the marked location, utilizing the open corner for easier access and completion of the dissection.

The surgeon examines the resected bowel specimen, pulling it over an instrument to assess its length and ensure the removal of bulbous dilated sections and redundancy. The specimen is visibly long, and the surgeon considers options for tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an isoparastaltic colocolonic anastomosis using a robotic stapler, dividing the distal and proximal bowel segments. The stapler successfully crosses both limbs with a single staple load, facilitating an end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon aligns the transverse colon with the distal descending colon, possibly the proximal sigmoid, and sews the two staple lines together using a stay suture of silk. The staple lines show no tension, and the angles are well aligned, facilitating a smooth anastomosis.

This case absolutely could have been done laparoscopic. I think that the robot just gives a little bit more control of things and you can't really beat the stability of this camera and how this anastomosis is being held while you're sewing. But I've certainly done my share of spleniflexure resections laparoscopically and if I didn't have the robot available on this day I would have had no problem doing this laparoscopically.

The surgeon creates a distal collotomy followed by a proximal collotomy, checking the perfusion of the inner lip to ensure proper enhancement. The surgeon then begins sewing away from themselves, taking full thickness bites to secure the anastomosis.
The surgeon navigates around the corner of the bowel and begins to canal back down, focusing on a specific pearl of tissue.

The surgeon begins sewing the inner layer of the anastomosis, starting in the middle and stitching towards themselves. After reaching around the corner, they plan to canal back up, ensuring precise alignment of the tissue layers.
The surgeon brings both sutures towards the middle to tie them together, ensuring a secure anastomosis. The original posterior suture on the staple line is brought out and around anteriorly.

The surgeon ties the silk suture, then moves the small bowel aside to examine the thick mesentery for any signs of bleeding. Cauterization is performed on some areas, and the descending colon is exteriorized through a fan and steel incision, visible near the location of robotic arm number three.
